title: Cyclobalanopsis saravanensis
intro: Cyclobalanopsis saravanensis, also known as Saravan chestnut, is a species of tree in the beech family (Fagaceae). This plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which may help to protect against various diseases, such as cancer, cardiovascular conditions, and infections. The bioactive constituents of Cyclobalanopsis saravanensis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often used to make teas, decoctions, and infusions, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, cough, and digestive problems.
taxonomy: Cyclobalanopsis saravanensis is classified within the family Fagaceae, which includes other species of beech trees and chestnuts. This species belongs to the genus Cyclobalanopsis, which is a group of evergreen trees native to Southeast Asia. Further, it is classified as a species of deciduous tree, specifically belonging to the subsection Glabrescentes. The exact classification is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Rosids, Order: Fagales, Family: Fagaceae, Genus: Cyclobalanopsis, Species: C. saravanensis. This classification places Cyclobalanopsis saravanensis among other tree species native to tropical regions.
